name: HAS-BLED
full_name: Hypertension, Abnormal liver/renal function, Stroke history, Bleeding predisposition, Labile INR, Elderly, Drug/alcohol usage
description: Estimates risk of major bleeding for patients on anticoagulation to assess risk-benefit in atrial fibrillation care.
purpose: Estimate the 1-year risk of major bleeding in patients with atrial fibrillation to assist in shared decision-making regarding anticoagulation.
when_to_use: As a reference tool to assist in the shared decision-making discussion regarding anticoagulation in patients with atrial fibrillation
Instead of, or in conjunction with, other bleeding risk scores such as HEMORR₂HAGES and ATRIA to determine risk of major bleeding in a patient with atrial fibrillation
Comparing the risk for major bleeding to the risk for thromboembolic events by the CHA₂DS₂-VASc to determine if the benefit of anticoagulation outweighs the risk
when_not_to_use: Use with caution in patients with chronic kidney disease – score has been shown to perform poorly
Use with caution in patients with cancer – score has been shown to perform poorly

items.label: renal_disease
items.description: Renal disease (dialysis, transplant, Cr >2.26 mg/dL or >200 µmol/L)

items.label: liver_disease
items.description: Liver disease (cirrhosis or bilirubin >2x normal with AST/ALT/AP >3x normal)

items.label: bleeding_predisposition
items.description: Prior major bleeding or predisposition to bleeding

items.label: labile_inr
items.description: Labile INR (unstable/high INRs, time in therapeutic range <60%)

items.label: medication_usage
items.description: Medication usage predisposing to bleeding (aspirin, clopidogrel, NSAIDs)

formula: hypertension + renal_disease + liver_disease + stroke + bleeding_predisposition + labile_inr + age_65 + medication_usage + alcohol_drug_use

interpretation.description: Low risk. Risk of major bleeding 0.9%. 1.13 bleeds per 100 patient-years. Anticoagulation should be considered.
Low risk. Risk of major bleeding 3.4%. 1.02 bleeds per 100 patient-years. Anticoagulation should be considered.
Moderate risk. Risk of major bleeding 4.1%. 1.88 bleeds per 100 patient-years. Anticoagulation can be considered.
High risk. Risk of major bleeding 5.8%. 3.72 bleeds per 100 patient-years. Alternatives to anticoagulation should be considered.
High risk. Risk of major bleeding 8.9%. 8.70 bleeds per 100 patient-years. Alternatives to anticoagulation should be considered.
High risk. Risk of major bleeding 9.1%. 12.50 bleeds per 100 patient-years. Alternatives to anticoagulation should be considered.
Very high risk. Scores greater than 5 were too rare to determine risk, but are likely over 10%.

references.citation: Pisters R, Lane DA, Nieuwlaat R, de Vos CB, Crijns HJ, Lip GY. A novel user-friendly score (HAS-BLED) to assess 1-year risk of major bleeding in patients with atrial fibrillation: the Euro Heart Survey. Chest. 2010 Nov;138(5):1093-100.

notes: The most important pitfall is using HAS-BLED as an absolute cut-off to withhold or withdraw anticoagulation. In the vast majority of AF patients, risk of stroke outweighs risk of bleeding. HAS-BLED should be used as an alarm bell to signal risk factors that can be avoided or reversed. The derivation study was based on a cohort in which only around 2/3 were on any antithrombotic therapy. Major bleeding was defined as intracranial hemorrhage, bleeding requiring hospitalization, bleeding with hemoglobin decrease >2 g/L, or bleeding requiring transfusion.
